Witness descriptions dating back decades portray them as small humanoid creatures with gray skin, oversized bald heads, and large black almond-shaped eyes. Reports often describe the beings as lacking visible ears, pronounced noses, or expressive facial features. The Grays became deeply embedded in popular culture after the famous Betty and Barney Hill abduction case in the 1960s. The New Hampshire couple claimed they were abducted by small gray-skinned beings aboard a spacecraft. The beings have appeared in countless movies, television series, and books, becoming the dominant image associated with extraterrestrials worldwide. Readers interested in UFO history can explore archived material through National UFO Reporting Center. Nordics: Human-Like Type of Aliens Linked to the Pleiades The so-called “Nordics” are described very differently from the Grays. According to UFO lore, these beings allegedly resemble tall, attractive humans with blond hair, blue eyes, and Scandinavian-like features. Reports describing Nordics date back to the 1950s and often portray them as peaceful and technologically advanced. Some UFO researchers claim the beings are connected to the Pleiades star cluster, located hundreds of light-years from Earth. Eric Davis reportedly told congressional staffers that Nordics are among the entities allegedly associated with unidentified aerial craft. Supporters of these theories often describe Nordics as benevolent beings attempting to guide humanity, though no scientific evidence supports those claims. Additional astronomy information about the Pleiades can be found at NASA Solar System Exploration. 123Next TAGS alien conspiracy theoriesalien speciesCIA scientistEric DavisextraterrestrialsGrays aliensHal PuthoffInsectoid aliensinterdimensional beingsnon-human intelligenceNordic aliensPentagon UFO filesreptilian aliensThe Age of DisclosureTim BurchettType of AliensUFO crash retrievalsUFO disclosureUFO hearingsunidentified aerial phenomenaUSA Herald Claims about different “Type of Aliens” secretly known to the U.S. government are once again fueling debate over UFO disclosure after former CIA-affiliated scientist Hal Puthoff stated that intelligence insiders allegedly encountered “at least four separate types” of non-human beings connected to recovered unidentified craft. Puthoff, who worked on controversial government psychic spy and UFO-related research projects during the Cold War era, made the remarks during an appearance on The Diary of a CEO Podcast alongside filmmaker Dan Farah. The comments come amid renewed public interest in UFOs and unidentified aerial phenomena following recent disclosure efforts in Washington and continued congressional hearings involving military whistleblowers and intelligence officials. According to Puthoff and physicist Eric Davis, the four alleged extraterrestrial groups most frequently discussed in classified circles are known as the Grays, Nordics, Insectoids, and Reptilians. Despite the sensational claims, the United States Department of Defense has repeatedly stated there is “no verifiable evidence” proving extraterrestrial craft or alien life has been recovered. More information about official government UFO investigations can be found at NASA UAP Research and Department of Defense All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office. Grays: The Most Famous Alien Species The “Grays” are perhaps the most recognizable alien beings in modern UFO culture. Witness descriptions dating back decades portray them as small humanoid creatures with gray skin, oversized bald heads, and large black almond-shaped eyes. Reports often describe the beings as lacking visible ears, pronounced noses, or expressive facial features. The Grays became deeply embedded in popular culture after the famous Betty and Barney Hill abduction case in the 1960s. The New Hampshire couple claimed they were abducted by small gray-skinned beings aboard a spacecraft. Puthoff and Davis suggested that descriptions from alleged government witnesses and crash retrieval accounts closely resemble those long associated with the Grays. The beings have appeared in countless movies, television series, and books, becoming the dominant image associated with extraterrestrials worldwide. Readers interested in UFO history can explore archived material through National UFO Reporting Center. Nordics: Human-Like Type of Aliens Linked to the Pleiades The so-called “Nordics” are described very differently from the Grays. According to UFO lore, these beings allegedly resemble tall, attractive humans with blond hair, blue eyes, and Scandinavian-like features. Reports describing Nordics date back to the 1950s and often portray them as peaceful and technologically advanced. Some UFO researchers claim the beings are connected to the Pleiades star cluster, located hundreds of light-years from Earth. Eric Davis reportedly told congressional staffers that Nordics are among the entities allegedly associated with unidentified aerial craft. Supporters of these theories often describe Nordics as benevolent beings attempting to guide humanity, though no scientific evidence supports those claims. Additional astronomy information about the Pleiades can be found at NASA Solar System Exploration. 123Next TAGS alien conspiracy theoriesalien speciesCIA scientistEric DavisextraterrestrialsGrays aliensHal PuthoffInsectoid aliensinterdimensional beingsnon-human intelligenceNordic aliensPentagon UFO filesreptilian aliensThe Age of DisclosureTim BurchettType of AliensUFO crash retrievalsUFO disclosureUFO hearingsunidentified aerial phenomenaUSA Herald Claims about different “Type of Aliens” secretly known to the U.S. government are once again fueling debate over UFO disclosure after former CIA-affiliated scientist Hal Puthoff stated that intelligence insiders allegedly encountered “at least four separate types” of non-human beings connected to recovered unidentified craft. The Witchy Girls take on Killing Heidi in hilarous drag supernatural comedy
Source: Q News Pty Ltd    Published: 2026-05-24 00:53:56

Screen Whoever denied funding for the supernatural drag comedy series, The Witchy Girls, must be truly kicking themselves. With new episodes dropping weekly, Lazy Susan and Zelda Moon shine with a host of queer creatives and comedy guests, including Elle Hooper, Judith Lucy, Sez, Greg Larson and more. Every time I've written about Lazy Susan and Zelda Moon's The Witchy Girls webseries, something hasn't felt quite right about using that term. Because, even though it is housed on Comedy Republic TV's YouTube Channel, the production values of the supernatural drag comedy are so much more than what one expects when they think ‘webseries'. Not to belittle the platform and the work people do on it, but The Witchy Girls is next level, in both production, comic timing, and Australian drag. This isn't a shock to anyone who knows of the many creative individuals and elements involved in the series creation. We are now halfway through the first season's run (because this show better get a second with actual funding, at least), and every time a new episode drops, it hits another level of comic nostalgia, queer entertainment and shares the tenacity of the Australian arts industry. Related content: Lazy Susan's drag-comedy ‘The Witchy Girls' has arrived Drag Race Down Under to directorial debut: Lazy Susan's heading to Cannes Lazy Susan feels immense responsibility with Drag Race Down Under win The drag-comedy webseries produced by Iris Arc Pictures was given full life while Lazy was travelling to film Drag Race Down Under vs The World overseas with Rhys Nicholson and their husband, Kyran. Lazy received the news that another of her projects had been rejected for funding. Kyran suggested bringing the work to audiences in the way comedy and drag started, live in the room, through Comedy Republic. Using live screenings and merch sales has allowed the series to fund itself after principal production was completed by a dedicated creative team, and this has recieved plenty of attention as the series launched, but I don't think enough attention is being given to the product itself. A comic series that just keeps getting better and better. Inspired by 90s television, The Witchy Girls follows the story of supernatural teens and best friends, Lazy Susan and Zelda Moon. The spell-casting pair navigate high school and the usual quirks of adolescence with ridiculously funny results that smash nostalgia and adult humour together. The series harks back to the early comedies of Adult Swim, meets 90s sitcoms and a touch of John Waters if he lived in Australia. What more could you ask for? Watching new episodes of The Witchy Girls ahead of their online release via live screening events is truly a treat, but that's nothing compared to the episodes themselves. Episode three sees Lazy and Zelda lose out on tickets to see Killing Heidi, and by attempting a powerful luck spell to change their fortune, everything hilariously spirals out of control. With Killing Heidi in the title of the ep, The Witchy Girls vs Killing Heidi, it wasn't a shock that lead singer Ella Hooper appears. But just like in the first two episodes, other talented guests surprise the viewer and perfectly hit their comedy mark. I'm used to seeing Sez armed with a guitar and a fearless attitude, but this time she wasn't armed with hilarious folk-pop songs; instead, she played the virgin leader of a high school improv troupe. Lazy and Zelda needed her blood to complete their latest ritual. What ensues is a series of catastrophically funny events that leave the Witchy Girls nowhere near their favourite band. Honestly, I don't want to spoil it, just watch the episode! The use of comic timing is EVERYTHING in episodes three and four. Not that they weren't before, but the production team and artists are firing on all cylinders. You can tell just how much heart, dedication and hard work are involved in weaving all these elements together to smash out comedy timing gold. It's a leap from the first two episodes (which were brilliant) and honestly makes me so excited to see where the remaining episodes will lead. When QNews spoke with Lazy Susan ahead of her Drag Race Down Under win, she told us, “One of the things that's a classic pain of my life is that if I do something, I want to do it in the most exuberant or silly way I can.” At the time, she was talking about the amazing creativity she brought to the Drag Race Runway, but that's an ethos the entertainer brings to everything she does. Lazy shared after her win that she felt “this immense responsibility to try and make it worth it, to make it a worthy win in what I'm able to do. Kind of going forward with my reign of terror.” She told us she wanted “to combine my love of filmmaking with my love of drag. If anyone's been watching any of my Instagram throughout the competition, that's kind of my soft launch into what I want to be doing.” “I want to get drag queens on screen and in the narrative. I want to start working on some of those projects, which you will hopefully see in the coming year.” And with The Witchy Girls, we certainly have, and it's not slowing down. The level of creativity in every element is next level, and as the episodes build, it shows just how hard Lazy will go when she puts her mind to it. After three sold-out live screenings, the team behind the show had no choice but to add an encore and bring audiences the whole damn thing. It's bigger, longer and uncut – with all six episodes playing back-to-back, hosted by Lazy and Zelda. Join them Friday, June 5, at Comedy Republic. You can get your tickets here. A post shared by The Witchy Girls – Awful Teens With Magic (@thewitchygirls) Sunday, May 24, the latest episode of The Witchy Girls drops on Comedy Republic TV on YouTube, and it is something truly special. Remember that episode of Charmed where Prue uses a spell to clone herself? Well, it's kind of like that, but so much better. Seriously, I almost wet my pants with laughter watching it. So much of the series promotion has been on Lazy Susan, which makes sense as she is the reigning Drag Race Down Under winner, but if you haven't already fallen in love with Zelda Moon, episode four is that moment. After watching the first four episodes of The Witchy Girls, it continues to grow from strength to strength. No doubt whoever denied this creative team funding for this project is truly kicking themselves. And we still have two more episodes to go! For the latest LGBTIQA+ Sister Girl and Brother Boy news, entertainment, and community stories in Australia, visit qnews.com.au. Find us on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram and YouTube. We aim to inspire and positively impact our readers, facilitating engagement and inclusivity within businesses, schools, families, and neighbourhoods.
